coredata.dc:title: Construction of 3D N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalyst to improve the photocatalytic performance of MOF-5 by changing the electron transfer path
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Xiangtao
Qiang, Taotao
Chen, Lu
Wang, Shaoting
coredata.dc:description: MOF-5 is an emerging semiconductor material with a great potential in photocatalytic wastewater treatment. However, its inherent deficiencies, such as the narrow range of photo-response, the fast recombination of photoinduced carriers and the unstable structure, largely limit its further application. In this work, super biocompatible N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) derived from the green precursor, coconut shells, were in situ grown on MOF-5 via a facile solvothermal method to construction of 0D/3D N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalyst with an enhanced photoreduction ability. A sustainable and pollution-free preparation process of N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalyst was delivered and biomass waste resource was effectively utilized via this method. This well-designed photocatalyst with the optimized 1% N-CQD loading presented a considerable photoreduction rate of Cr (VI), which was 2.5 times higher that of pure MOF-5. It also possessed a strong structural stability and reusability even after multiple tests. The boosting photocatalysis activity could be attributed to the doping of N-CQD, who not only act as an electron acceptor to separate the photoexcited electrons and holes effectively, but also play a vital role as a photosensitizer to improve the visible-light absorption. Furthermore, the influence of activation methods on the photocatalytic activity of MOF-5 were also investigated theoretically. For the purpose of clarifying the potentially photocatalytic mechanism of N-CQD/MOF-5 in photoreduction Cr (VI), a series of tests involving photoelectrical characterizations and fluorescence spectroscopy were executed. Therefore, this study provides an innovative photocatalytic system for an enhanced photoreduction performance of Cr (VI) of MOF-5.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis activity
Metal-organic framework materials
N-doped carbon quantum dots
Cr (VI) photoreduction

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Key Scientifific Research Group of Shaanxi Province ( 2020TD -009 ), Key Scientific Research Program of Shaanxi Provincial Education Department( Collaborative Innovation Center project ) ( 20JY003 ), Science and Technology Plan Project of Xi'an Weiyang District (No. 201907 ) and the Youth Innovation Team of Shaanxi Universities .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A cubic cage within the MOF-5 structure (H atoms omitted for clarity.).
FT-IR spectra and (b ~ f) XPS plots of MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5: (b) total spectrum, (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s, (f) Zn2p spectra.
(a) XRD patterns of the samples. (b) XRD patterns of recycling experiments of N-CQD/MOF-5.
(a–c) SEM images of MOF-5; (d–h) SEM images of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5; (i–k) TEM images of MOF-5, 2- N-CQD/MOF-5.
(a–f) EDX element mapping of N-CQD/MOF-5: (c) N, (d) C, (e) O, (f) Zn.

(a) Photocatalytic activity of MOF-5,2-N-CQD/MOF-5; (b) Photocatalytic activity of different samples; (c) Photocatalytic activity of MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5 treated by different activation methods. (d) Recycling experiments of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves of MOF-5, 2-N-CQD/MOF-5.
(a) UV–vis DRS of the samples, (b) band gap spectra, (c) Fluorescence spectrum of as-prepared sampels (d) XPS valence band spectrum of MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5.
(a) EIS spectrum of MOF-5 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5, (b) I-t curves of MOF-5 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5.
A schematic illustration of photocatalytic reduction Cr(VI).

head.ce:title: Construction of 3D N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalyst to improve the photocatalytic performance of MOF-5 by changing the electron transfer path

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi'an, Shaanxi, 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Bioresources and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi'an, Shaanxi, 710021, P.R.China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Light Chemistry Engineering Education, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi'an, Shaanxi, 710021, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Bioresources and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi'an, Shaanxi, 710021, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MOF-5 is an emerging semiconductor material with a great potential in photocatalytic wastewater treatment. However, its inherent deficiencies, such as the narrow range of photo-response, the fast recombination of photoinduced carriers and the unstable structure, largely limit its further application. In this work, super biocompatible N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) derived from the green precursor, coconut shells, were in situ grown on MOF-5 via a facile solvothermal method to construction of 0D/3D N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalyst with an enhanced photoreduction ability. A sustainable and pollution-free preparation process of N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalyst was delivered and biomass waste resource was effectively utilized via this method. This well-designed photocatalyst with the optimized 1% N-CQD loading presented a considerable photoreduction rate of Cr (VI), which was 2.5 times higher that of pure MOF-5. It also possessed a strong structural stability and reusability even after multiple tests. The boosting photocatalysis activity could be attributed to the doping of N-CQD, who not only act as an electron acceptor to separate the photoexcited electrons and holes effectively, but also play a vital role as a photosensitizer to improve the visible-light absorption. Furthermore, the influence of activation methods on the photocatalytic activity of MOF-5 were also investigated theoretically. For the purpose of clarifying the potentially photocatalytic mechanism of N-CQD/MOF-5 in photoreduction Cr (VI), a series of tests involving photoelectrical characterizations and fluorescence spectroscopy were executed. Therefore, this study provides an innovative photocatalytic system for an enhanced photoreduction performance of Cr (VI) of MOF-5.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid global industrialization progress has intensified people's concern to the issue of industrial wastewater discharge. Toxic and hazardous organic pollutants enter the ecosystem along with wastewater disposal. Due to its biological toxicity and in-degradability, it has posed a huge threat to both human health and the balance of the biological chain [1]. Industries such as tanning and electroplating produce wastewater containing trivalent and hexavalent chromium, which has been confirmed to exist in the tap water system [2]. It is well-known that hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)) is toxic and has been categorized as one of the most dangerous ions in the world. Therefore, how to effectively dispose the Cr (VI) containing sewage has emerged as a research emphasis in water remediation field [3]. Considerable attempts and purified technologies have been performed for removing Cr (VI) from water, such as adsorption [4], flocculation [5] and membrane filtration [6], the effectiveness of the above methods is still unsatisfied.

Solar energy, a renewable and sustainable energy. Converting solar energy into chemical energy to reduce or degrade pollutants is considered as a potential solution to the problem of hexavalent chromium pollution [7,8]. At present, semiconductor photocatalysts have manifested desirable potential in photoreduction Cr (VI) owing to their vital superiorities, such as low cost, environment friendly and high efficiency in using solar energy [9–11]. In order to obtain a photocatalyst with higher activity, multiple types of semiconductor materials have been explored to date. For instance, the application of metal oxides, metal sulfides, graphite carbonitrides, metal-organic framework materials, graphene and bismuth-based materials in the field of photocatalysis have been reported in prior studies [12–16]. Among which, metal organic framework (MOF), as a representative semiconductor material with a three-dimensional porous structure, has aroused more and more attention recently.

MOF is composed of organic ligands and metal ions [17]. Originated from its porous structure, its relatively large surface area is conducive to the diffusion of pollutants and heavy metal ions, making MOF an ideal platform for photocatalytic centers. MOF-5, who has a three-dimensional extended porous structure with a typical cubic lattice, is a typical MOF material with Zn2+ and terephthalic acid (H2BDC) as its central ion and organic ligand. As shown in Fig. 1 , there are Zn4O units at each cubic corner [18], which are formed by bridging 6 carboxyl groups with benzene rings. MOF-5 is also regarded as a promising photocatalyst as unsaturated metal atoms or organic linkers can act as carrier transmission paths after being excited by light [19]. In comparison with traditional semiconductors, MOF-5 presents an advanced property for its adjustable structure. Thus, the utilization of MOF-5 in the field of photocatalysis has become the focus of the developing of efficient and controllable photocatalysts. In 2007, García and her colleagues [20] first used MOF-5 as a photocatalyst for phenol degradation under visible light, providing experimental evidence supporting MOF-5 as a useful semiconductor photocatalyst. Nevertheless, confined to the existence of large band gap, the scarcity of light absorption rate restricts the commercialization of current MOF-5 material [21]. Meanwhile, the intrinsic obstacles such as the low conductivity, and the fast photoexcited electron-hole recombination, also set up barriers for MOF-5 in photocatalysis using.

Up to now, massive endeavors have been devoted to the modification of MOF-5. For example, the organic ligands adjustment, such as NH2-UiO-66 [22], NH2-MIL-101 [23], Al-MIL-101-X (X = –CH3, –NO2, –OCH3, –C6H4, –F2, -(CH3)2, -(OCH3)2) [24], the novel metal doping, such as Pd@MOF-5 [25] and Ag/AgCl@MIL-53-Fe [26], Co-MOF [27], and the multidimensional materials construction, such as CNT@MOF-5 [28], MIL-53(Fe)/Bi12O17Cl2 [29] and polymer-MOF [30]. In recent years, the carbon-based materials, represented by graphene and carbon nanotubes, have been successfully compounded with MOF-5 and effectively improved its photocatalytic efficiency [31]. However, the complex experiment processes as well as the high costs urged researchers to explore another preferential carbon-based material as a substitute to modified MOF-5.

Carbon quantum dot (CQD), an excellent zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based material, is widely used as electron transfer intermediary in photocatalytic for its low resistivity and high chemical stability [32]. Notably, N-doped carbon quantum dot (N-CQD) possesses a greater ability to harvest and scatter light compared to pristine CQD as the introduced amino group (-NH2) can convert ultraviolet light into visible light to harness a broad solar spectrum. For example, Di, J., et al. [33] constructed N-CQD/BiOBr ultrathin nanosheets through in-situ synthesis and improved the molecular oxygen activation ability of BiOBr under visible light irradiation. Similarly, based on the combination of CQD and other materials, a series of heterojunction photocatalysts are synthesized, such as N-CQD/SnS2 [34] and CQDs/ZnIn2S4 [35], CuCo2O4/CQDs [36]. Thus, N-CQD is emerged as an appropriate co-catalyst in the fabrication of novel photocatalysts. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are few reports on modifing MOF-5 with N-CQD for achieving a tremendous photocatalytic activity.

In this paper, a novel 0D/3D photocatalyst N-CQD/MOF-5 was synthesized by in-situ grown N-CQD on MOF-5 via a controllable solvothermal method. N-CQD was obtained from coconut shells in order to maximize the use of biomass waste resources for the purpose of environment protection. Through this method, the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of N-CQD could guide the formation of MOF crystals and provide binding sites for the formation of MOF crystal nuclei, thereby achieving an intimate connection between MOF-5 and N-CQD interfaces. The introduction of N-CQD effectively enhanced the visible-light absorption capacity of MOF-5, drastically facilitated the photoinduced charges transportation behaviors, and subsequently enhanced the visible-light-driven photocatalytic performance of N-CQD/MOF-5. The innovative synergy effect of MOF-5 and N-CQD exhibits a boosting photoreduction of Cr (VI) and thus possesses a fresh strategy for industrial sewage disposal. Compared with the previous work, in this paper, we use biomass waste resources as raw materials, the preparation method is simple and efficient, and can be mass-produced, the preparation method is simple and efficient, and can be mass-produced, and has achieved the highly efficient treatment of Cr(VI) wastewater, which enhances the practical application possibility of photocatalysis.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) (A.R.), urea (A.R.), diphenyl carbonyl hydrazine (A.R.), terephthalic acid (99%), concentrated sulfuric acid (98.0%), phosphoric acid (A.R.), N,N dimethylformamide (DMF) (A.R.), potassium dichromate (A.R.), acetone (A.R.), methyl alcohol (A.R.), hydrochloric acid (38%). The above reagents utilized in our study were purchased from Aladdin Chemical. The water used during the experiment was deionized by the laboratory water purify system.

The pristine MOF-5 was synthesized as a reference. Zn(NO3)2•6H2O (0.8g) and H2BDC (0.4g) were dissolved in DMF (65 mL) at room temperature. Kept stirring for 30 min for thorough mixing, then the above solvent was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. The white crystals obtained after the reaction were filtered out and washed several times with DMF to remove unreacted zinc salts and H2BDC. After which, the washed crystals were heated in ethanol at 90 °C to get activated, and re-added ethanol 2–3 times during heating to avoid evaporation and promote the complete activation of the sample. Ethanol was filtered out after activation. MOF-5 could be obtained by drying the above samples under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. To investigate the effect of the activation temperature on the photocatalytic activity of MOF-5, the contrastive samples were prepared, and labeled as MOF-5-1, indicating the activation process of MOF-5 was carried out at room temperature instead of at 90 °C.

N-CQD was synthesized by hydrothermal method with coconut shells as C source and urea as N source (details could be found in Supporting Information). N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalyst was prepared by in-situ synthesis, as shown in Fig. 2 . Zn(NO3)2•6H2O (0.8g) and 0.4g of H2BDC were dissolved in DMF(65 mL), kept stirring (30 min) for fully dissolving, then added a certain amount of N-CQD, continued magnetic stirring until the solvent turned from transparent to dark red. The above solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and heated at 150 °C for 10 h. The activation method and drying process of the above samples were the same as those of MOF-5 synthesis. The resultant samples were labeled as X-N-CQD/MOF-5, where “X” is 1, 2, 3, 4, indicating N-CQD/MOF-5 with N-CQD loading rates of 0.5%, 1%, 2%, and 3%. By adjusting the reaction temperature and the activation temperature, the contrast samples based on 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 were prepared, and labeled as 2-N-CQD/MOF-5-1 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5-2. In which, the former indicates the reaction temperature of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 was 130 °C, while the latter one presents the 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 sample was activated at room temperature.

The photocatalytic property of all catalysts was assessed by photoreduction tests of Cr (VI) in the photochemical reactor. Photocatalysts (0.05 g) were added into 50 mg/L Cr (VI) solution (50 mL). Dark absorption was maintained for 30 min with stirring to reach the absorption-desorption equilibrium. Xe lamp was carried out as the visible-light source, kept the distance between the light and the device 5.8 cm to get the best illumination effect. During the experiment, approximately 1 mL of the suspension was extracted at predetermined time intervals and centrifuged for further testing. Diphenyl carbazide spectrophotometry (DPC) was used to determine the Cr (VI) concentration in the solution (details could be found in Supporting Information). The photoreduction rate of Cr (VI) could illustrate the photocatalytic ability of N-CQD/MOF-5.

The chemical structure of as-prepared samples was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker Alpha VECTOR-22, Germany) using KBr disk as a reference at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was obtained by using AXIS SUPRA instrument (UK). X-Rays Diffraction (XRD) characterization (Bruker D8 Advance) was used to obtain the crystallographic structure information, with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at the scan rate of 5° min−1. The morphology of above composites was investigated by Hitachi S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20S-TWINFEI, the accelerating voltage: 200 KV) was carried out to exhibit the microstructure and details of the as-synthesized samples. Furthermore, by analyzing the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm, the relevant surface area and pore distribution data were obtained on the ASAP2460 (USA) instrument. The photochemical property was analyzed by UV–vis-DRS spectrometer (Agilent Technologies Cary series) using BaSO4 as the reference. And the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was obtained by a fluorescence spectrometer (FS5, Edinburgh) at an excitation wavelength of 270 nm.

To investigate the photoelectrochemical (PEC) properties, electrochemical tests were performed at electrochemical workstation (PARSTAT 4000). A three-electrode system was established to test electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent response curves [37]. Na2SO4 solution (0.5mol/L−1) was used as the electrolyte, and platinum (Pt) electrode, ITO glass (15 × 15 × 1.1 mm), and Ag+/AgCl electrode were used as the counter electrode, working electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The working electrodes were prepared by ultrasonically dispersing the equal samples in equivalent ethanol containing a small amount of Nafion, the dispersion was evenly coated on the conductive surface of ITO glass and then dried at room temperature.

FT-IR spectra were first recorded to analyze the chemical structure and the existing interaction between the functional groups of the photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the primary characteristic peak located at 1655 cm−1 could be attributed to the CC bond vibration. And the broad absorption bands from 3600 to 3000 cm−1, could be assigned to the O–H and N–H bond vibration. The distinguished peak centered at 1400 cm−1, corresponding to the C–N stretching vibration, which indicates the successfully introduction of –NH2 in N-CQD [38]. The infrared spectrum of MOF-5 showed a strong peak at 1573 cm−1, which was caused by the stretching vibration of the carboxylate anion existed in the material, lower than the CO stretching vibration in free carboxylic acid. No characteristic peak of –COOH group was found in the range of 1760–1690 cm−1, indicating that the –COOH groups were deprotonated when 1, 4-phthalic acid reacted with metal ions. Notably, in the spectrum of N-CQD/MOF-5, it can be seen that the vibration peak was located at 3168 cm−1, which is attributed to the formation of –C(O)–NH- bond for the reaction between the amino groups in N-CQD and the carboxyl groups in MOF-5 [39]. The characteristic absorption peak at 549 cm−1 could be attributed to the Zn–O bond in the crystal cluster of zinc oxide, which further proved the existing of MOF-5 in the composites. The FT-IR spectra well indicated the successful incorporation of N-CQD/MOF-5.

In Fig. 3b, XPS analysis was carried out to study the surface chemical elemental composition of the pristine MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5. Three main peaks located at 286.7, 530.73, and 1021.37 eV could be observed in the figure, which could be assigned to C 1s, O 1s, and Zn 2p respectively [40]. To further explain the functional group distribution, the high-resolution XPS spectra of MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5 were depicted as shown in Fig. 3c to f. The C 1s spectrum of two photocatalysts, as shown in Fig. 3c, can be decomposed into three components, namely C–C group (~284.81ev, 58.19%), C–O group (~285.06ev, 25.3%), and CO group (~288.88ev, 16.51%) [41]. Distinctly, two different oxygen-containing free metal groups (CO and C–O) presented in both spectra [42], they were expected to provide sufficient reaction sites for the photocatalytic process. The characteristic peak, which located at 401.2 eV, in N 1s core spectra (Fig. 3d) can be assigned to the C–N–C bond in N-CQD/MOF-5 compared with MOF-5 [43]. The distinguished peak located at 531.9 eV in O 1s core spectra (Fig. 3e), corresponding well to the –C(O)–NH- bond formed between MOF-5 and N-CQD in the composite, evidently displayed the successful involving of N-CQD in the as-synthesized samples [44]. Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 3f) illustrated two strong peaks centered at 1022.8 and 1046.1 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 respectively, indicating the Zn2+species are in a completely oxidized state [45]. After loading with N-CQD, the Zn 2p3/2 bond of N-CQD/MOF-5 was divided into two parts, in which the binding energy of the peak at 1024.5 eV slightly decreased and a new peak appeared, which can be attributed to the change of bond structures between Zn and other atoms existed in the sample, further demonstrates that Zn may participate in the interaction of two components.

The crystallographic structure of as-prepared samples was analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the XRD pattern of MOF-5 was consistent with the standard spectrum, illustrates the successful synthesis of the crystalline phase of MOF-5, while the sharp peak indicates the high degree of crystallinity [46]. In the spectrum of N-CQD/MOF-5, two peaks located at 2θ = 6.9° and 9.8°, indicates the crystalline phase of MOF-5 remained after the combination [47]. Besides, a new peak at 2θ = 28.5° (marked as R), indicates the introduction of N-CQD has changed the crystal plane phase of the composites. Notably, compared with the sharp and high-intensity peaks of synthesized composites, the XRD curve of N-CQD sample was flat and wide, which could be assigned to the amorphous structure of N-CQD. Compared with MOF-5, the peak location of N-CQD/MOF-5 showed a slight shift, which may attribute to the formation of N-CQD/MOF-5 interface. The higher degree of crystallinity reveals the fewer defects in the crystal lattice, which is conducive to the behavior of charges transportation and separation. With more electrons migrating to the surface of photocatalysts, more free radicals would generate for a superior photocatalytic efficiency [48].

The SEM images of MOF-5 presented a typical regular octahedral cube structure with a porous morphology, which were consistent with the previous literature reports [49] (Fig. 5 a–c). The SEM images of the representative composite (2-N-CQD/MOF-5) were displayed in Fig. 5d-h, the MOF-5 crystal matrix could be clearly observed, and a large number of small particles (N-CQD) were attached on the surface of the matrix intimately. Theoretically, the small particle size peculiar to the 0D structure makes it possible for N-CQD to enter the pores on the surface of MOF-5 to form a void structure. The formation of space structure effectively enlarges the specific surface area and improves the adsorption capacity of MOF-5. Compared with MOF-5, 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 has a rougher surface with gaps, which were left by N-CQD when it entered MOF-5. Meanwhile, the structure of MOF-5 has not been destroyed in the process, maintaining the original cubic shape, and the two were combined through functional groups, which effectively improved the stability of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5. The TEM images of MOF-5 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 were shown from Fig. 5i to k. The TEM of MOF-5 presented a cube lamellae structure, which was consistent with SEM results that MOF-5 presents an octahedral microstructure. It can be seen from Fig. 5e that the matrix of the composite material exhibited a lamellar structure with small particles attached to the surface (Fig. 5f and g). The monodisperse distribution of N-CQD can be seen in Fig. 5h, presented the average size of 2–5 nm. From the TEM of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5, it can be seen that the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were closely combined with MOF-5, which was consistent with the results of SEM. Such tight bonds are beneficial to the chemical stability of the material. EDX spectrum in Fig. S4 revealed the existence of C, N, O and Zn elements on the surface of N-CQD/MOF-5, which was consistent with the results presented by XPS. Furthermore, HAADF-STEM and EDX mapping (Fig. 6 a–f) both indicated the uniform distribution of each element on the 0D/3D N-CQD/MOF-5 framework. Integrating the above characterizations, it can be considered that the composite has been well synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method, N-CQD is evenly distributed and closely contacted with MOF-5.

The photocatalytic performance was tested by the photocatalytic experimental device, and the reaction temperature was maintained through the condensation device to avoid the influence of thermal catalysis and to prevent the loss of Cr (VI) solution due to high temperature (Fig. S2). The photoreduction research of Cr (VI) solution without catalysts under light irradiation was performed as a blank experiment. The flat curve shown in Fig. 7 a demonstrates the original photoreduction ability of Cr (VI) is neglectable, further reveals the reduction rate of Cr (VI) could be mainly assigned to the photoreduction ability of photocatalysts. The synthesis conditions of MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5 were optimized and the photoreduction tests of Cr (VI) were carried out under the same condition (Figs. S3a–f). It could be seen in Fig. 7a that the photoreduction efficiency of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 was about 2.5 times that of pure MOF-5 under 1 h irradiation. Photocatalytic activity curves of different samples were depicted in Fig. 6b, it is worth noting that with the addition of N-CQD, the efficiency of photoreduction increased first and then decreased, which illustrates the appropriate amount of N-CQD is of paramount significant in photocatalytic experiments to achieve the optimal effect. While the overload of N-CQD may cause the surface of MOF-5 over-covered, weak the internal connection and subsequently affect the photoreduction ability.

The influence of activation method on the photoreduction ability of MOF-5 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 was discussed by changing the activation temperature, as displayed in Fig. 7c. The photocatalytic activity of MOF-5 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 (both activated at 90 °C) were higher than that of MOF-5-1 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5-2 (both activated at room temperature) (Fig. 7c). Therefore, it can be concluded that high-temperature activation is more beneficial to the improvement of photocatalytic efficiency of catalysts. The possible reason is that higher temperature is conducive to the reaction of ethanol and guest molecules remaining in the catalyst's channels to avoid the occurrence of channels blockage.

Besides, the stability and reusability of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 were evaluated by five cycle tests under the same experimental conditions as shown in Fig. 7d. The results revealed that the photocatalytic reduction rate of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 slightly decreased after 5 cycles, which could be attributed to the formation of Cr (OH)3 during the reduction. However, the decline still can be ignored in general. XRD (Fig. 4b) was performed to investigate the crystal and structure stability of recycled 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 during this process. The barely changed intensities and locations of characteristic peaks illustrate the synthesized photocatalyst is stable and reusable. So far, 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalysts have been proven to be high-efficiency and have a great potential in large scale application.

To determine the physical properties of MOF-5 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5, N2 adsorption−desorption experiment was carried out, and the results were shown in Fig. 8 . The well-defined Langmuir-type shape indicates the microporous structure of composite as well as the uniformity of pores which is in agreement with the structural model of cavities in MOF-5, in which the passage of spheres of diameters up to 8.0 A° has been confirmed [50]. The specific surface areas of MOF-5 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 could be calculated by BET (Brunauer, Emmett and Teller) as shown in Table S1, which were 69.33 and 430.7 m2/g, respectively. In addition, the pore volume of as-synthesized samples was also summarized in Table S1. The specific surface area of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 is 6.2 times that of MOF-5, indicating the composite is more active sites in charge transportation. Meanwhile, the isotherm of MOF-5 presented a typical type-II adsorption curve, while that of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 belongs to type-I adsorption curve. This illustrated that after loading N-CQD, the macropore structure of original material turned to the small pore structure. N-CQD were partly loaded on the surface of MOF-5 and partly entered into the interior, which roughed the surface structure of MOF-5 and complicated the internal pore structure, thus improved the specific surface area of the composite material. This is consistent with SEM results, and this change is beneficial to the photocatalytic process.

UV–vis DRS was used to study the light absorption performance and band gap energy of as-prepared samples. Fig. 9 a shows the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of N-CQD, MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5. It can be seen from the figure that the original MOF-5 exhibited selective absorption at 200–400 nm. After loading N-CQD, the spectrum showed a significant red-shift. With the increase of N-CQD loading, the red-shift was more obvious. And the light absorption edge broadened from ultraviolet light to visible light. The addition of N-CQD significantly enhanced the visible light absorption intensity of composites. However, according to the above reduction experiment results, with the increase of N-CQD loading, the photoreduction capacity did not improve continuously, which indicated that the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts was not only affected by the light absorption ability, but also related to other factors, such as the crystal structure of catalysts, the aggregation of photoinduced charge carriers.

The band gap energy of different materials was calculated according to the formula: ɑhv = A (hv-Eg)n/2, the corresponding band gap (Eg) of MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5 were calculated to be 3.54 and 3.41 eV (Fig. 9b), and the relative valence band maximum (VBM) of MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5 were detected to be 3.29 and 3.09 eV by XPS-VB spectra, respectively(Fig. 9d). The formula (ECB = EVB –Eg) was used to estimate the conduction band positions (ECB), the results were determined to be −0.45 eV and −0.12 eV for MOF-5 and N-CQD/MOF-5. According to the calculation, N-CQD/MOF-5 has a more positive valence band and a more negative conduction band compared with MOF-5, which illustrated a reduced energy required for electronic transition, thus improving the utilization of visible light.

Hereafter, fluorescence spectrum (PL) analysis was performed at an excitation wavelength of 270 nm to study the recombination rate of photo-generated electrons and holes. As shown in Fig. 9c, the pure MOF-5 sample exhibited the strongest emission peak at 470 nm. In comparison, the fluorescence peak intensity of other N-CQD/MOF-5 samples significantly decreased, which indicates the reduced recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes in photocatalysts. Furthermore, with the increase of N-CQD doping, the recombination rate decreased first and then increased, indicating the excessive loading might be unfavorable to the photocatalytic process as the carriers would recombine rapidly.

The interfacial charge transfer behavior of MOF-5 and 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 was further confirmed by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). EIS analysis in Fig. 10 a showed that the Nyquist arc radius of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 reduced significantly compared with the pristine MOF-5, which could be attributed to the existence of N-CQD, indicates the small charges transferring resistance in 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 [51]. The results of EIS stay the same with the PL testing, suggesting that the appropriate injection of N-CQD could significantly promote the formation of heterostructure internal and largely benefit the conduction of charges. The photocurrent measurement was used to reflect the interface charge transfer activity. The higher photocurrent intensity often leads to a better photocatalytic performance. It can be observed in Fig. 10b that the transient photocurrent response of 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 was higher than that of MOF-5 under visible-light irradiation under several on-off cycles, indicating that 2-N-CQD/MOF-5 holds a stronger ability in generating and transferring the photoexcited charge carriers under irradiation. Additionally, the transient photocurrent response performance was compared with other MOF-based catalysts as listed in Table S2, while our research shows its prominent superiority [52–54].

Based on the above experimental studies, a possible mechanism of N-CQD improving the photocatalytic performance of MOF-5 materials was proposed. As shown in Fig. 11 , in the experiment of Cr (VI) photoreduction, after being excited by the visible-light, the benzene ring acts as a photon absorber, who can effectively transport electrons to inorganic metal cluster part. In N-CQD/MOF-5, the introduced –NH2 in N-CQD can convert ultraviolet light absorption into visible light absorption to expand the light utilization rate and the light response edge of MOFs. Under irradiation, the excited MOF-5 generates electrons and holes, and the electrons transfer smoothly to N-CQD, which can be regraded as a photon acceptor, effectively slowing down or inhibiting the recombination of electrons and holes, thereby improving the degree of chemical reaction and enhancing the photocatalytic ability.

Photocatalytic technology is considered to be one of the most promising methods for wastewater treatment. In this paper, in view of the poor photoresponse, the fast uniting of lightinduced carriers, and the unstable structure of MOF-5. N-CQD, which was prepared from coconut husks, was combined with MOF-5 by solvothermal method to synthesis N-CQD/MOF-5 as a novel photocatalyst. The results indicate that N-CQD loading effectively improves the photoreduction performance of MOF-5. Under simulated visible-light irradiation, the photocatalytic performance of the optimized N-CQD/MOF-5 composite is 2.5 times that of the original MOF-5, which could be attributed to the addition of N-CQD as an electron acceptor, which can effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers, and subsequently enhance the light adsorption, as well as the photoreduction ability to the target pollutants. Therefore, this study provides a potential method to improve the photocatalytic performance of MOF and comes up with a superior photocatalyst for Cr(VI) reduction.

Xiangtao Qin: Visualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, preparation and, Writing – review & editing. Taotao Qiang: Conceptualization, Supervision, and, Writing – review & editing. Lu Chen: Writing – original draft, preparation and, Writing – review & editing. Shaoting Wang: Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Key Scientifific Research Group of Shaanxi Province (2020TD -009), Key Scientific Research Program of Shaanxi Provincial Education Department(Collaborative Innovation Center project) (20JY003), Science and Technology Plan Project of Xi'an Weiyang District (No. 201907) and the Youth Innovation Team of Shaanxi Universities.
